summary:
Histories are used to depict the distribution of continuous variables through frequency over intervals; they differ from bar or pie charts which describe categorical rather than quantitative data.

The final choice based on this rationale is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

Okay, I'm now going to summarize only what was said directly within Agent1’s reasoning—without adding interpretation beyond their own words—and keep it strictly two sentences focused solely on clinically-relevant facts related to identifying how continuous variables are displayed.

Histograms are used to represent continuous variables because they display frequencies across intervals, illustrating the distribution of measurable outcomes like time or lab results. Unlike bar charts, pie diagrams, or Pareto plots—which relate to categorical data—the histogram uniquely captures the full range and pattern of continuous numerical data.
